sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what changed
before = last_transition.before_frame.segmentation
after = last_transition.after_frame.segmentation

before_nodes = {n['id']: n for n in before['nodes']}
after_nodes = {n['id']: n for n in after['nodes']}

print(f"Before: {len(before['nodes'])} nodes, After: {len(after['nodes'])} nodes")
for nid, n in after_nodes.items():
    if nid in before_nodes:
        bn = before_nodes[nid]
        if n['boundary'] != bn['boundary'] or n['color'] != bn['color'] or n['pixels'] != bn['pixels']:
            print(f"Node {nid}: color={bn['color']}->{n['color']}, pixels={bn['pixels']}->{n['pixels']}")
            print(f"  Before: {bn['boundary']}")
            print(f"  After:  {n['boundary']}")
    else:
        print(f"Node {nid} NEW: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

for nid, n in before_nodes.items():
    if nid not in after_nodes:
        print(f"Node {nid} DISAPPEARED: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Check if the purple square moved
print()
print("Current purple nodes:")
for n in after['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'p':
        print(f"  Node {n['id']}: pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.content:
Now I see the pattern! When I clicked on the gray dot at [55, 8]:
1. Node 23 (purple square at [52,9]-[54,11]) → became a gray dot (G, 1 pixel) at [53, 10]
2. Node 24 (gray dot at [55, 8]) → became a purple square (p, 9 pixels) at [54,7]-[56,9]
So the purple square "moved" from one position to the next gray dot along the path! The old position became a gray dot, and the clicked gray dot became the new purple square position.
This means the game is about moving the purple square along the path of gray dots. The goal is likely to move it all the way to the blue blob at the top-right.
The path of gray dots goes from bottom-left to top-right:
- Node 24: [55, 8] (just clicked, now purple)
- Node 25: [57, 6]
- Node 22: [51, 12]
- Node 21: [49, 14]
- Node 20: [47, 16]

- Node 10: [27, 36]
- Node 9: [25, 38]
- Node 8: [23, 40]
- Node 7: [21, 42]
- Node 6: [19, 44]
- Node 5: [17, 46]
Wait, but the purple square is now at [54,7]-[56,9], which is node 24's original position [55, 8]. The path goes from bottom-left to top-right. The next dot after [55, 8] going upward would be [51, 12] (node 22).
Actually, let me reconsider the path order. The dots form a diagonal line. Looking at the coordinates:
- [57, 6] - node 25 (lowest, leftmost)
- [55, 8] - node 24 (clicked)
- [53, 10] - was the white cross, now gray dot (node 23)
- [51, 12] - node 22
- [49, 14] - node 21
- ...
- [17, 46] - node 5 (highest, rightmost, closest to blue blob)
So the path goes from bottom-left [57, 6] to top-right [17, 46]. The purple square is currently at [55, 8] (node 24). The next step would be to click on [51, 12] (node 22) to move the purple square further along the path.
I should batch-click all the remaining dots along the path to move the purple square to the blue blob!
Let me compute the sequence of clicks needed.

<parameter=code>
# The purple square moves along the path when I click the next gray dot!
# I need to click all remaining gray dots in sequence to reach the blue blob.
# The path from current position [55,8] upward to the blue blob:
# [51,12], [49,14], [47,16], [45,18], [43,20], [41,22], [39,24], [37,26], [35,28], [33,30], [31,32], [29,34], [27,36], [25,38], [23,40], [21,42], [19,44], [17,46]

path_dots = [
    (51, 12), (49, 14), (47, 16), (45, 18), (43, 20), (41, 22), (39, 24), (37, 26),
    (35, 28), (33, 30), (31, 32), (29, 34), (27, 36), (25, 38), (23, 40), (21, 42),
    (19, 44), (17, 46)
]

# Batch click all remaining dots
actions = [{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': r, 'col': c} for r, c in path_dots]
action(actions)
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
